dialogue.utterance: EMS Unit 6, respond to 987 Oak for a seizing patient with a 35 year-old male.
Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911?
He was mopping and then collapsed with a seizure. They said he'd had five already.
Hey sir, can you respond to your name? Let's check.
He opens his eyes to verbal stimulation, but doesn't seem alert.
Pupils are 3mm and reactive bilaterally.
Sir, can you say your name for me?
I'm fine.
Do you know where you are right now?
He doesn't answer. Just says "I'm fine" and won't engage otherwise.
I don't see any command response. He localizes to pain but doesn't obey verbal motor instructions.
That's correct. I documented localized motor response in the notes.
Airway is clear. No signs of obstruction or unusual secretions.
No specific posture is needed to maintain airway. Monitor for any postictal complications.
Let's confirm respiratory rate and effort now.
Respiratory rate is 16, unlabored. No expiratory wheeze or crackles.
Bilateral equal breath sounds. No sign of distress.
Pulse is 86, strong and regular. Skin is warm, dry, no signs of cyanosis.
Blood pressure is 148 over 84. No signs of pallor or JVD.
No signs of respiratory distress. Breathing is observed to be comfortable.
Want a quick medical history from anyone present. Any known conditions?
Epilepsy, depression, chronic pain, and spinal stenosis.
Does he have any known allergies to food, drugs, or other substances?
Tomato allergy. No drug allergies reported so far.
What medications does he take regularly?
Phenytoin, phenobarbital, nortriptyline, and diclofenac.
When was his last meal or drink?
He was mopping. No details on exact time of last intake.
Sir, any pain since your seizures began?
No pain reported postictally so far. Assessment deferred until lucid.
Skin is intact with no rash or bruising currently assessed. Mild drooling noted after the seizure episode.
Any inequality of pupils or facial asymmetry?
Pupils are equal, reactive to light, and symmetrical. Face appears normal.
Any neck pain or stiffness? Swallowing trouble?
He didn't complain of anything during the seizure. Only in the ER afterward.
Feel his extremities: pulses strong, no swelling, or deformity noted.
Responds only with "I'm fine" when eyes open, no further communication.
Documented two new seizures lasting 30-35 seconds. Grinding teeth and drooling noted.
Pulse remains at 86, no change since the last check.
Respiratory rate still 16, no signs of distress.
Blood pressure is now noted at 118 over 77.
Attempting to recheck blood sugar. Not accessible due to device limitation.
No blood sugar measurement obtained, readings nonviable.
SpO2 reads 99%. Normal saturation maintained.
Reapplying 4-lead ECG for continuous monitoring now.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with no ectopy or other changes.
Subsequent glucose check also reads zero-same as previous.
Temperature obtained is 98.4°F. No fever noted.
IV access established at left antecubital. Checking site integrity.
No complications noted since initial IV placement.
Confirmed GCS score is 11: eyes to voice, incoherent speech, localizing to pain.
Head and neck checked again: no jugular distension noted.
Medical protocol for seizure-protocol 3-12. Alert command if needed.
Subsequent BP noted 125 over 75 with no changes reported.
IV remains secure. No fluids or meds administered.
Patient remains unchanged. No additional interventions needed.
Sheriff's request directs us to [COMPANY_NAME] System for evaluation.
All straps applied. Stretcher is safely loaded.
Ensuring all straps are secure and patient is safely held in place.
Rechecking temperature stays consistent: still 98.4°F.
35 y/o with seizure history, ongoing meds on board: phenytoin and others.
GCS at 11. IV in place, blood pressure trends stable. No additional events during transport.
ER later noted head, neck, and back pain, which was not in the field assessment.
Patient has a normal temperature, skin, and breathing during transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the final transport destination with sheriff's authorization.
All straps and restraints are rechecked before initiating transport.
No movement against restraints noted. Everything prepared accordingly.
Keeping 4-lead monitoring on for any changes in rhythm during the transport.
Rechecking SpO2 remains at 99%. No changes in oxygen saturation.
Previous interventions observed to be stable and well-documented.
Observation remains at 16, regular breathing with no distress signs.
Rerunning terminal SpO2, response remains unchanged.
Checking IV for patency, no issues with flow or positioning.
No observed deformities, swelling, bruising, or rashes noted during the process.
All interventions held in close check, nothing required at this point.
Enroute to [COMPANY_NAME] without incident or change in patient status.
The patient remained uncommunicative and postictal the entire time.
Head and neck exams unremarkable, no swelling, deformity, or throat issue seen.
Transport destination remains [COMPANY_NAME] as directed by [CITY] Sheriff's Deputy.
All strapping devices are in use. No compromise during insertion.
Transport stabilized and secured with full assurance of patient safety.
Patient reported no distress during the procedure once secure was confirmed.
Directing in line with protocol 3-12 for medical handling.
All site notes in accordance with postictal documentation for protocol.
Repeat glucose readings remain nonviable, addressing en route to ER.
Final check shows temperature stays at 98.4°F. No fever.
All transport duties completed to high level of patient care and safety.
IV remains patent and secure for transport arrival.
No movement or postictal complications noted since loading.
All strapping devices are in use, both anterior and posterior. No compromise during insertion.
Patient is in ER bed being treated on attendance.
Records transferred and full report given to nursing staff.
Transport complete. All procedures documented post-assessment.
No autonomy from patient assessed en route. Notes act as field indicator.
Final verification that all transport positioning is safely used.
[COMPANY_NAME] assisted with placement of patient upon arrival.
Stratagonically advised postictal handling is conducted during transfer.
Final pulse check is 83 bpm. No other changes in status since last reading.
Transport finalized. No signs of distress or seizures post-loading.
All care functions performed to highest EMT standard with no incidents recorded.
